Last summer, Anna took a train for the first time. She went with her father to a small town by the sea. The trip took three hours. Anna sat by the window and watched the fields and hills go by.

At the station, the air smelled like salt. Anna and her father walked ten minutes to the beach. The water was cold, but Anna put her feet in it and laughed. Her father bought two ice creams from a little shop near the sand.

In the afternoon, they collected white shells and built a small sand castle. Anna put the best shell in her pocket. On the train home, she fell asleep with the shell in her hand. She wanted to come back next summer.

questions.q: What is the story mostly about?
options: A girl who learns to swim in cold sea water
A father who works at a small train station
A girl's first train trip to a town by the sea
A family that moves to a new house near a beach
questions.explanation: 글 전체가 안나가 처음으로 기차를 타고('took a train for the first time') 바닷가 마을에 다녀온 하루를 다룹니다. 수영 배우기, 역에서 일하는 아버지, 이사는 본문에 없습니다.

questions.q: Why did Anna most likely keep the shell in her hand on the train home?
options: It helped her remember her happy day at the sea
She planned to sell it at a shop in her town
Her father told her to keep it safe in her own hand
She wanted to give it to a friend from school
questions.explanation: 안나는 가장 좋은 조개껍데기를 주머니에 넣었고 'She wanted to come back next summer'라고 했으므로, 행복한 하루를 기억하려고 쥐고 있었다고 추론할 수 있습니다. 판매·아버지의 지시·친구 선물은 근거가 없습니다.

questions.q: How long did the train trip take?
options: One hour
Two hours
Ten minutes
Three hours
questions.explanation: 본문에 'The trip took three hours'라고 명시되어 있습니다. 10분은 역에서 해변까지 걸어간 시간입니다.

questions.q: What did Anna's father buy near the sand?
options: Two white shells
Two ice creams
Two train tickets
Two cold drinks
questions.explanation: 본문에 'Her father bought two ice creams from a little shop near the sand'라고 나옵니다. 조개껍데기는 산 것이 아니라 함께 주운 것입니다.

questions.q: What can we guess about Anna at the end of the story?
options: She did not like the long train ride
She was afraid of the cold sea water
She enjoyed the trip and hoped to go again
She lost her best shell on the way home
questions.explanation: 마지막 문장 'She wanted to come back next summer'에서 여행을 즐겼고 다시 가고 싶어 한다는 것을 알 수 있습니다. 차가운 물에는 발을 담그고 웃었으므로 무서워하지 않았습니다.

gist.q: Which sentence best gives the main idea of the passage?
options: A father teaches his daughter to swim at the beach.
A girl enjoys her first train trip to a sea town.
A family opens a small ice cream shop near the sand.
A girl finds a lost shell and gives it back to her father.

scan.q: What color were the shells Anna and her father collected?

validation_note: 본문만으로 5문항+gist를 직접 풀어 정답이 유일함을 확인했습니다(수동 검산). 실측 125단어로 티어1(A2) 범위(110~160) 내이고, 상위 기초 어휘·단문 위주라 라벨에 적합합니다. '정답=유일 최장'은 Q4 1개뿐이라 편당 1개 이하 규칙을 지키며, scan 'white'는 본문에 실제 등장합니다. 자연스러운 원어민 영어로 판단해 무수정 통과.

title: The Busy Squirrel
text:
In many parks, you can see small gray squirrels. They run up and down the trees and look very busy. In autumn, they are the busiest animals in the park.

Why are they so busy? Squirrels eat nuts, and winter is coming. In winter, there is not much food. So in autumn, squirrels collect nuts and hide them in many places. They put some under the ground and some in holes in trees.

A squirrel cannot remember every hiding place. It finds many of its nuts again, but it loses some too. The lost nuts stay under the ground. In spring, some of them grow into new trees. So when a squirrel forgets a nut, it plants a tree without knowing it.

questions.q: What is the passage mostly about?
options: How squirrels build warm homes in old trees
Why squirrels hide nuts and what happens to them
How people can feed gray squirrels in a city park
Why squirrels sleep under the ground all winter
questions.explanation: 글은 다람쥐가 가을에 도토리를 숨기는 이유(겨울 대비)와 잊힌 도토리가 나무가 되는 결과를 설명합니다. 집짓기, 사람의 먹이 주기, 겨울잠은 본문에 없습니다.

questions.q: Where do squirrels hide their nuts?
options: In flower gardens and under dry leaves
In warm places inside people's houses
Deep under the water and in the tall grass
Under the ground and in holes in trees
questions.explanation: 본문에 'They put some under the ground and some in holes in trees'라고 명시되어 있습니다. 꽃밭, 집 안, 물속은 본문에 없는 장소입니다.

questions.q: Why is there a problem for squirrels in winter?
options: There is not much food during the winter
The tall trees slowly lose all their holes
The city parks all close in the cold weather
Other park animals often steal their homes
questions.explanation: 본문에 'In winter, there is not much food'라고 나옵니다. 그래서 가을에 미리 도토리를 모아 숨기는 것입니다. 나머지 보기(나무 구멍이 사라짐·공원 폐쇄·집을 빼앗김)는 본문에 없는 진술입니다.

questions.q: Why does the writer say a squirrel 'plants a tree without knowing it'?
options: Because squirrels learn to plant trees in spring
Because people plant trees where squirrels live
Because a forgotten nut can grow into a new tree
Because squirrels know where every nut is hidden
questions.explanation: 잊힌 도토리가 땅속에 남아 'In spring, some of them grow into new trees'라고 했으므로, 다람쥐가 의도하지 않아도 나무를 심는 셈이 됩니다. 다람쥐가 심는 법을 배우거나 모든 위치를 기억한다는 내용은 본문과 반대입니다.

questions.q: What can we guess about a squirrel's memory?
options: A squirrel never forgets a hiding place
A squirrel remembers only holes in trees
A squirrel asks other squirrels for help
A squirrel remembers many places, but not all
questions.explanation: 본문의 'A squirrel cannot remember every hiding place. It finds many of its nuts again, but it loses some too'에서 많은 곳은 기억하지만 전부는 기억하지 못한다는 것을 알 수 있습니다.

gist.q: Which sentence best gives the main idea of the passage?
options: Squirrels hide nuts, and lost nuts become new trees.
Squirrels are the fastest animals in every city park.
People should give nuts to hungry squirrels in autumn.
Old trees with holes are good homes for park animals.

scan.q: In which season do lost nuts grow into new trees?

validation_note: 수동 검산 결과 원본 Q2가 '정답=유일 최장'이었고 Q4까지 합해 편당 2개가 되어 규칙(편당 1개 이하) 위반이었습니다. Q2의 네 보기를 재작성해 정답('There is not much food during the winter', 40자)이 더 이상 유일 최장이 아니게 만들고 오답도 본문에 없는 구체 진술로 유지했습니다. 이제 유일-최장은 Q4 1개뿐입니다. 실측 122단어로 티어1 범위 내, scan 'in spring'은 본문에 등장, 정답 인덱스는 0~3에 분산됩니다.

title: Sara Takes the Bus
text:
Sara moved to a big city with her family last month. Everything was new: the tall buildings, the busy streets, and the loud sounds. On Friday, Sara had to visit her aunt on the other side of the city. Her mother could not go with her, so Sara took the bus alone for the first time.

She waited at the bus stop near a bakery. The number 7 bus came, and Sara sat behind the driver. She held a small map in her hand and counted the stops. An old woman next to her saw the map and smiled. "Are you going to Green Park?" she asked. "Yes," said Sara. "Then get off with me at the next stop."

Sara thanked the woman and got off the bus. Her aunt was waiting at the park gate. Sara felt taller that day. The big city did not seem so big anymore.

questions.q: What is the story mostly about?
options: A woman who drives the number 7 bus every day
A family that opens a bakery near a bus stop
A girl who gets lost in a big city at night
A girl's first bus ride alone in her new city
questions.explanation: 글 전체가 사라가 새 도시에서 처음으로 혼자 버스를 타고('took the bus alone for the first time') 이모를 만나러 가는 이야기입니다. 버스 기사, 빵집 개업, 길을 잃는 내용은 중심이 아닙니다.

questions.q: Why did Sara count the stops on the bus?
options: She wanted to tell the driver where to stop
She was afraid of missing her stop
The old woman asked her to count them
She was playing a number game with her aunt
questions.explanation: 사라는 새 도시에서 처음 혼자 버스를 탔고 'held a small map in her hand and counted the stops'라고 했으므로, 내릴 곳을 놓칠까 봐 세었다고 추론할 수 있습니다. 기사에게 말하기, 할머니의 부탁, 놀이라는 근거는 없습니다.

questions.q: Where did Sara wait for the bus?
options: At the gate of Green Park
In front of her aunt's house
At a bus stop near a bakery
On the other side of the city
questions.explanation: 본문에 'She waited at the bus stop near a bakery'라고 명시되어 있습니다. Green Park의 문은 이모가 기다리던 곳입니다.

questions.q: Who was waiting for Sara at the park gate?
options: Her aunt
Her mother
The old woman
The bus driver
questions.explanation: 본문에 'Her aunt was waiting at the park gate'라고 나옵니다. 어머니는 함께 갈 수 없었고, 할머니는 버스에서 함께 내린 사람입니다.

questions.q: What does "Sara felt taller that day" mean in the story?
options: Sara grew very fast in the big city
Sara felt strong and sure of herself
Sara was the tallest person on the bus
Sara wanted to be as tall as her aunt
questions.explanation: 혼자 버스를 타는 일을 해낸 뒤 'The big city did not seem so big anymore'라고 이어지므로, 키가 아니라 자신감이 커졌다는 뜻으로 보는 것이 자연스럽습니다.

gist.q: Which sentence best gives the main idea of the passage?
options: A kind woman shows a girl around a city bakery.
A girl visits Green Park with her whole family.
A girl rides the bus alone and grows braver.
An aunt teaches a girl how to read a city map.

scan.q: Which number bus did Sara take?

validation_note: 본문만으로 5문항+gist를 직접 풀어 정답이 유일함을 확인했습니다(수동 검산). 실측 150단어로 티어1 범위(110~160) 내입니다. '정답=유일 최장' 문항이 0개라 균형 규칙을 여유 있게 지키고, 관용 표현('felt taller') 추론은 뒤 문장 'The big city did not seem so big anymore'로 정답이 하나로 갈립니다. scan 'number 7'은 본문에 등장하며 자연스러운 원어민 영어로 판단해 무수정 통과.

title: Why Your Body Needs Sleep
text:
Sleep is not just rest. When you sleep, your body does important work. Your muscles grow and repair. Your brain cleans itself and stores what you learned in the day.

Most children need about ten hours of sleep each night. Many adults need seven or eight hours. When people do not sleep enough, they feel tired and slow. They forget things easily and get angry fast. Some people also get sick more often, because sleep helps the body fight germs.

You can sleep better with a few simple steps. Go to bed at the same time every night. Keep your room dark and quiet. Do not look at bright screens late at night, because the light tells your brain to stay awake. A short walk in the afternoon can also help you fall asleep at night.

questions.q: What is the passage mostly about?
options: How bright screens harm children's eyes
Why sleep matters and how to sleep well
Why adults need more sleep than children
How muscles grow when people exercise
questions.explanation: 글은 잠이 몸과 뇌에 하는 일('your body does important work')과 잘 자는 방법을 함께 다룹니다. 화면이 눈을 해친다는 내용은 없고, 어른이 아이보다 더 자야 한다는 진술은 본문(아이 ~10시간, 어른 7~8시간)과 반대입니다.

questions.q: What would probably happen if someone watched videos on a bright screen at midnight?
options: The person may stay awake longer
The person's muscles may repair faster
The person may sleep more deeply that night
The person may remember the videos better
questions.explanation: 본문에 'the light tells your brain to stay awake'라고 되어 있으므로, 밤늦게 밝은 화면을 보면 더 오래 깨어 있게 된다고 추론할 수 있습니다. 근육 회복이나 깊은 잠은 본문 내용과 맞지 않습니다.

questions.q: What can we guess about a student who sleeps only five hours every night?
options: The student may grow taller than friends
The student may need less food each day
The student may forget things and feel tired
The student may fight germs better than before
questions.explanation: 잠이 부족하면 'they feel tired and slow. They forget things easily'라고 했으므로, 다섯 시간만 자는 학생은 피곤하고 잘 잊어버릴 것이라 추론할 수 있습니다. 나머지 보기는 근거가 없거나 본문과 반대입니다.

questions.q: About how many hours of sleep do most children need each night?
options: About six hours
About seven hours
About eight hours
About ten hours
questions.explanation: 'Most children need about ten hours of sleep each night'라고 명시되어 있습니다. 일곱~여덟 시간은 어른에게 필요한 양입니다.

questions.q: What does the passage say can help you fall asleep at night?
options: Going to bed at a different time each night
Taking a short walk in the afternoon
Looking at a bright screen late at night
Keeping the room light and full of sound
questions.explanation: 'A short walk in the afternoon can also help you fall asleep at night'라고 명시되어 있습니다. 나머지 보기는 본문이 권하는 것(같은 시간에 자기, 어둡고 조용한 방, 화면 피하기)과 반대입니다.

gist.q: Which sentence best gives the main idea of the passage?
options: Children sleep more than adults because they play outside more.
Bright screens are the main reason why people get sick so often.
Sleep helps the body and brain, and good habits improve it.
A dark bedroom is the only thing people need for good sleep.

scan.q: How many hours of sleep do many adults need?

validation_note: 검증 완료: 본문 136단어(A2 기초 어휘, 단순 시제)로 티어1 범위 내. 5문항+gist 모두 본문만으로 정답이 유일함을 재확인(오답은 본문 소재를 이용한 틀린 진술로 상식 소거 불가). scan.answer 'seven or eight hours' 본문 존재 확인. 정답=유일최장 문항 0개, 정답 인덱스 1/0/2/3/1(gist 2)로 분산.

title: Ben's Juice Stand
text:
Last summer, Ben wanted to buy a book about space, but he did not have enough money. His aunt said, "You can sell orange juice in front of the house." Ben liked the idea.

First, Ben used his three dollars to buy oranges, sugar, and paper cups. His aunt explained, "This money is your cost. You must earn it back before you make any real money."

Ben sold each cup for one dollar. On the first day, he sold four cups. He was happy, but his aunt showed him the numbers. Four dollars minus three dollars of cost was only one dollar of profit.

The next day, Ben moved his table near the busy park. He sold ten cups. This time his cost was four dollars, so his profit was six dollars. After one week, Ben had enough money for the book. He also learned a big lesson: where you sell matters as much as what you sell.

questions.q: What is the story mostly about?
options: A boy who reads many books about space at the busy park
An aunt who opens a juice shop in front of her house
A boy who plants orange trees to earn some money
A boy who sells juice and learns about cost and profit
questions.explanation: 이야기 전체가 벤이 주스를 팔면서 비용(cost)과 이익(profit)을 배우는 과정입니다. 공원에서 책을 읽거나, 이모가 가게를 열거나, 나무를 심는 내용은 본문에 없습니다.

questions.q: How much money did Ben use to buy oranges, sugar, and cups at the start?
options: One dollar
Three dollars
Four dollars
Six dollars
questions.explanation: 'Ben used his three dollars to buy oranges, sugar, and paper cups'라고 명시되어 있습니다. 1달러는 컵 하나의 가격, 4달러와 6달러는 둘째 날의 비용과 이익입니다.

questions.q: What did Ben do differently on the second day?
options: He sold each cup for a higher price
He bought fewer oranges to cut his cost
He moved his table near the busy park
He asked his aunt to sell the juice for him
questions.explanation: 'The next day, Ben moved his table near the busy park'라고 나옵니다. 컵 값은 첫날과 같은 1달러였고(10컵×1달러=10달러 매출), 재료를 줄이기는커녕 둘째 날 비용은 4달러로 늘었습니다.

questions.q: What did Ben's aunt most likely want him to understand on the first day?
options: Not all the money he got was real profit
He should stop selling juice and save his money
He needed to buy a bigger table for the juice
Four cups a day was more than most shops sell
questions.explanation: 이모가 'Four dollars minus three dollars of cost was only one dollar of profit'이라는 계산을 보여 준 것은, 번 돈에서 비용을 빼야 진짜 이익이 남는다는 점을 알려 주려는 뜻으로 추론됩니다.

questions.q: If Ben sells juice again next summer, what will he most likely do?
options: Sell in front of the quiet house again
Wait for his aunt to give him the money
Spend his money on a book before selling
Set up his table near a busy place
questions.explanation: 벤은 'where you sell matters as much as what you sell'이라는 교훈을 얻었고, 실제로 붐비는 공원 근처에서 더 많이 팔았습니다. 따라서 다시 판다면 사람이 많은 곳을 고를 것이라 추론할 수 있습니다.

gist.q: Which sentence best tells the main idea of the story?
options: A boy learns that a good place to sell helps him earn more.
A boy finds out that orange juice costs too much money to make.
An aunt teaches a boy that books are better than money.
A boy discovers that selling juice is harder than reading books.

scan.q: How many cups of juice did Ben sell on the first day?

validation_note: 검증 완료: 본문 158단어로 범위 내. 금액 계산(4-3=1, 10-4=6, 컵값 항상 1달러) 일관성 재확인. 5문항+gist 모두 본문 근거로 정답 유일. Q1(정답 'Three dollars')만 유일최장이나 보기 폭 10~13자로 미미하며 편당 1개 이하 규칙 충족. scan.answer 'four cups' 본문 존재 확인.

title: The Bag by the Door
text:
Dan often forgot things in the morning. One day he left his homework on his desk. Another day he forgot his gym shoes. His teacher was not happy, and Dan felt bad.

Dan's father gave him a simple idea. "Every night after dinner, pack your bag and put it by the front door," he said. "Do it at the same time every day. Soon you will not need to think about it."

At first, Dan needed a note on his door to remember the new plan. After two weeks, something changed. His hands packed the bag while his mind thought about other things. The new habit was now part of his evening, like brushing his teeth.

Dan stopped forgetting his things. He also learned something useful: a habit grows strong when you repeat it at the same time and in the same place.

questions.q: What is the story mostly about?
options: A teacher who gives too much homework to her class
A father who packs a school bag for his son every night
A boy who stops forgetting things by building a habit
A boy who learns to brush his teeth after every dinner
questions.explanation: 이야기는 물건을 자주 잊던 댄이 저녁마다 가방을 싸는 습관을 만들어 문제를 고치는 과정입니다. 아버지는 방법을 알려 줬을 뿐 대신 싸 주지 않았고, 이 닦기는 비유('like brushing his teeth')로만 나옵니다.

questions.q: What did Dan's father tell him to do?
options: Write his homework list on the classroom desk
Ask his teacher to send a note home every day
Get up much earlier and pack his bag in the morning
Pack his bag after dinner and put it by the door
questions.explanation: 아버지가 'Every night after dinner, pack your bag and put it by the front door'라고 말했습니다. 아침에 일찍 일어나라거나 선생님께 부탁하라는 내용은 본문에 없습니다.

questions.q: What helped Dan remember the new plan at first?
options: A note on his door
An alarm on his clock
A call from his teacher
A list from his father
questions.explanation: 'Dan needed a note on his door to remember the new plan'이라고 명시되어 있습니다. 알람, 선생님 전화, 아버지의 목록은 본문에 없습니다.

questions.q: What does the story mean when Dan's hands packed the bag while his mind thought about other things?
options: Dan was too tired to think about his school
The habit was now easy and needed no thought
Dan packed the wrong things into his bag
Dan still needed his father's help each night
questions.explanation: 'His hands packed the bag while his mind thought about other things'는 생각하지 않아도 몸이 저절로 할 만큼 습관이 쉬워졌다는 뜻입니다. 바로 뒤에 습관이 저녁의 일부가 되었다고 나옵니다.

questions.q: Based on the story, which plan would best help a girl who forgets to water her plant?
options: Water it a lot on one day of the month
Move the plant to a new room each week
Ask her friends to remind her from time to time
Water it right after breakfast every day
questions.explanation: 본문의 교훈은 'a habit grows strong when you repeat it at the same time and in the same place'입니다. 매일 아침 식사 직후라는 같은 시간·같은 흐름으로 반복하는 계획만 이 원리에 맞고, 나머지는 반복이 드물거나 장소를 계속 바꿉니다.

gist.q: Which sentence best gives the main idea of the story?
options: A father shows his son that homework matters more than sports.
Repeating an action at the same time builds a strong habit.
A note on the door is the best way to remember school things.
Boys who forget their gym shoes make teachers very unhappy.

scan.q: What did Dan forget besides his homework?

validation_note: 검증 완료: 본문 143단어로 범위 내(A2 어휘·단문). 5문항+gist 모두 본문 인용 근거로 정답 유일, 동시 정답 보기 없음 재확인(Q4 오답은 반복 빈도·장소 조건 위반으로 소거). 정답 인덱스 2/3/0/1/3(gist 1)로 분산, 정답=유일최장 문항 0개. scan.answer 'his gym shoes' 본문 존재 확인.

title: The Art of Packing Light
text:
Many travelers believe that a bigger suitcase means a better trip. Experienced travelers often say the opposite. A small bag, they argue, can make a journey easier and more enjoyable.

The reasons are simple. With only a light backpack, you can walk straight out of the airport instead of waiting at the baggage belt. You can climb the stairs of an old guesthouse without help, or run for a train at the last minute. If your plans change, you change with them.

Packing light takes practice. The trick is to choose clothes that all match each other, so a few pieces can make many outfits. Washing a shirt in a hotel sink at night takes five minutes, while carrying a week of extra clothes tires you out all day. Seasoned travelers also leave space in the bag when they leave home, because they know they will find things worth bringing back.

In the end, a small bag teaches a bigger lesson. The less you carry, the more attention you have for the places and people around you. For many people, that is the real purpose of travel.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: How to find cheap flights for long journeys
Why carrying less luggage can improve a trip
How to wash clothes properly in a hotel room
Why big suitcases are safer at busy airports
questions.explanation: 글 전체가 짐을 줄이면 여행이 어떻게 쉬워지는지(공항 통과, 계단, 계획 변경 등)를 설명하므로 1번이 정답입니다. 세탁은 요령 중 하나로만 잠깐 언급됩니다.

questions.q: What can be inferred about travelers with large, heavy suitcases?
options: They may find it harder to change plans quickly
They can usually leave the airport faster than others
They spend more time washing clothes at night
They bring home more gifts than light packers
questions.explanation: 본문은 가벼운 가방이면 '계획이 바뀌면 함께 바뀔 수 있다(If your plans change, you change with them)'고 했으므로, 반대로 무거운 짐은 계획 변경을 어렵게 한다고 추론할 수 있습니다. 0번이 정답입니다.

questions.q: According to the passage, what is the deeper lesson of traveling with a small bag?
options: Travelers should buy fewer souvenirs when abroad
Cheap trips are more fun than costly ones
Carrying less lets you notice more around you
Trains are a better choice than airplanes
questions.explanation: 본문 마지막에 '덜 가지고 다닐수록 주변 장소와 사람에게 더 많은 주의를 둘 수 있다(The less you carry, the more attention you have)'고 했으므로 2번이 정답입니다.

questions.q: What does the passage say about washing a shirt at night?
options: It ruins delicate clothes after a few washes
It costs extra money at nearly every hotel
It works well only with special travel soap
It takes about five minutes in a hotel sink
questions.explanation: 본문에 '호텔 세면대에서 밤에 셔츠를 빠는 데 5분이 걸린다(Washing a shirt in a hotel sink at night takes five minutes)'고 명시되어 있으므로 3번이 정답입니다.

questions.q: According to the passage, what is the trick to packing fewer clothes?
options: Buying new clothes in every city you visit
Wearing the same outfit for the whole trip
Choosing pieces that all match one another
Packing only clothes made for warm weather
questions.explanation: 본문은 '서로 모두 어울리는 옷을 고르는 것(choose clothes that all match each other)'이 적은 옷으로 여러 차림을 만드는 요령이라고 했으므로 2번이 정답입니다.

gist.q: Which sentence best states the main idea of the passage?
options: Airports are the most stressful part of any journey.
Travelers should always buy clothes at their destination.
A week of extra clothes keeps travelers ready for anything.
Packing less makes travel freer and more rewarding.

scan.q: Where does the passage say you can wash a shirt at night?

validation_note: 검증 완료: 본문만 근거로 5문항+gist를 다시 풀어 전 문항 정답이 유일함을 확인했습니다. 본문 187단어로 티어2(B1) 범위(140~200) 내이며, '정답=유일 최장' 문항은 0/6, scan.answer('in a hotel sink')는 본문에 그대로 등장합니다. 오답도 본문 소재(세탁·계획변경·짐공간)를 활용해 구체적으로 쓰였고 상식만으로는 소거되지 않습니다. 수정 없음.

title: The City Before Sunrise
text:
Most people think a city sleeps at night, but a city never really stops. Between four and six in the morning, while the streets look empty, a hidden shift of workers is already busy.

Bakers are usually the first. Bread dough needs hours to rise, so many bakeries turn on their ovens around three o'clock. Next come the market workers, who unload trucks full of vegetables, fish, and flowers before the shops open. Street cleaners follow, sweeping away the traces of the night before. Bus drivers start their engines while the sky is still dark, because the earliest passengers — nurses, cooks, and security guards — are already waiting at the stops.

This early world has its own small society. The same faces meet every morning: a driver greets a cleaner, a baker hands a warm roll to a market worker. Many of them say they enjoy the quiet hours, when the air is cool and the roads are clear.

By the time most citizens wake up, this shift is finishing. The fresh bread, the clean streets, and the running buses seem to appear by themselves. In truth, they are gifts from people whose workday is already half over.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: Why bread must be baked before the sun rises
How cities can reduce noise late at night
The hidden workers who prepare the city each dawn
The best time of day for citizens to ride city buses
questions.explanation: 글 전체가 새벽에 도시를 준비하는 보이지 않는 노동자들(제빵사, 시장 노동자, 청소부, 버스 기사)을 다루므로 2번이 정답입니다. 빵과 버스는 그중 한 사례일 뿐입니다.

questions.q: What can be inferred about the nurses, cooks, and security guards in the passage?
options: They prefer walking to work instead of riding buses
They finish their jobs before the bakers even start theirs
They earn more money than the morning bus drivers
Their working days begin while most people are asleep
questions.explanation: 본문은 하늘이 아직 어두울 때 '가장 이른 승객들(nurses, cooks, and security guards)'이 이미 정류장에서 기다린다고 했으므로, 이들의 근무가 대부분의 사람이 잠든 시간에 시작된다고 추론할 수 있습니다. 3번이 정답입니다.

questions.q: Why do the fresh bread and clean streets seem to 'appear by themselves'?
options: Most citizens are still asleep while the work is done
Machines now handle almost all of the early morning jobs
The workers hide because they dislike being watched
Shops secretly prepare everything the evening before
questions.explanation: 본문은 대부분의 시민이 깰 무렵 이 새벽 근무가 끝나 간다고 했습니다. 일이 이루어지는 동안 시민들이 자고 있어 결과물만 보게 되므로 0번이 정답입니다.

questions.q: Around what time do many bakeries turn on their ovens?
options: Around one o'clock
Around three o'clock
Around five o'clock
Around six o'clock
questions.explanation: 본문에 '많은 빵집이 3시쯤 오븐을 켠다(turn on their ovens around three o'clock)'고 명시되어 있으므로 1번이 정답입니다.

questions.q: What do the market workers unload from the trucks?
options: Vegetables, fish, and flowers
Bread, milk, and fresh eggs
Newspapers, mail, and packages
Fruit, cheese, and cold meat
questions.explanation: 본문에 시장 노동자들이 '채소, 생선, 꽃(vegetables, fish, and flowers)'이 가득 실린 트럭을 내린다고 했으므로 0번이 정답입니다.

gist.q: Which sentence best captures the central point of the passage?
options: City buses should run more often during the night hours.
Hidden early workers keep the city running before dawn.
Bakers work harder than anyone else employed in the city.
Empty streets at night prove that modern cities truly sleep.

scan.q: What does a baker hand to a market worker?

validation_note: 검증 완료: 본문만 근거로 다시 풀어 5문항+gist 정답이 유일함을 확인했습니다. 본문 197단어로 티어2 범위 내, scan.answer('a warm roll')는 본문에 그대로 등장합니다. '정답=유일 최장' 문항은 Q4 하나뿐(길이차 2자, 18~20)으로 전체 1/3 상한 이내입니다. 오답의 시각·품목(1/5/6시, bread·newspapers·fruit 등)은 본문을 읽어야만 갈리도록 구성되어 상식 소거가 불가능합니다. 수정 없음.

title: The First Snow Call
text:
When Dana moved to another city for work, she and her best friend Ruth made a strange promise. Whoever saw the first snow of winter would call the other, no matter the hour. It began as a joke on the day Dana packed her boxes, but neither of them forgot it.

The first year, Ruth called at two in the morning. Dana answered with a sleepy voice, then stood by her window for half an hour, listening to Ruth describe the snow falling on their old street. The next winter, snow reached Dana's city first, and it was her turn to wake Ruth.

Over the years, the calls grew longer. They talked about new jobs, difficult neighbors, and small victories that seemed too minor to mention in a normal phone call. Somehow, the snow made it easier to speak honestly.

Ten winters have passed since Dana moved away. The two friends still have never missed a first snow. Ruth says the promise works because it is simple: it does not ask for much, only that once a year, someone thinks of you first.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: A yearly snow promise that keeps two friends close
A woman who regrets moving to a different city
The heavy snowstorms that hit two nearby cities
Two friends who argue about a forgotten phone call
questions.explanation: 글 전체가 첫눈이 오면 서로에게 전화하기로 한 약속으로 10년간 우정을 이어 온 두 친구의 이야기이므로 0번이 정답입니다. 이사에 대한 후회나 다툼은 나오지 않습니다.

questions.q: Why were the friends able to share 'small victories' during the snow calls?
options: They had promised to report every small detail of their weeks
Ruth kept notes about Dana's life throughout the year
The calls created a space where honest talk felt natural
Snow reminded them of promises they made as children
questions.explanation: 본문은 평소 통화라면 꺼내기 애매한 작은 일들을 이야기했고 '눈이 솔직하게 말하기를 더 쉽게 만들었다(the snow made it easier to speak honestly)'고 했으므로 2번이 타당합니다.

questions.q: What does the story suggest about long-distance friendships?
options: They usually fade away within about ten years
They survive only when friends visit often
They need expensive gifts to stay strong
A small, steady habit can keep them alive
questions.explanation: 10년간 이어진 단순한 약속 하나가 우정을 지켰고, Ruth도 '많은 것을 요구하지 않아서(it does not ask for much)' 약속이 통한다고 했으므로 3번이 맞습니다. 방문이나 선물 이야기는 본문에 없습니다.

questions.q: Who made the very first snow call, and when?
options: Dana, at midnight on a snowy evening
Ruth, at two o'clock in the morning
Dana, on the day she packed her boxes
Ruth, on the morning after a snowfall
questions.explanation: 본문에 '첫해에는 Ruth가 새벽 2시에 전화했다(The first year, Ruth called at two in the morning)'고 명시되어 있으므로 1번이 정답입니다. 짐을 싸던 날은 약속이 농담으로 시작된 날일 뿐 전화한 날이 아닙니다.

questions.q: How long have the friends kept the promise without missing a first snow?
options: For about two winters
For about five winters
For about seven winters
For about ten winters
questions.explanation: 본문에 'Dana가 떠난 뒤 열 번의 겨울이 지났다(Ten winters have passed)'고 했고 한 번도 첫눈을 놓치지 않았다고 했으므로 3번이 정답입니다.

gist.q: Which sentence best states the main idea of the passage?
options: A simple snow promise has kept two friends close for years.
Dana learned that moving to a new city always ends old friendships.
Ruth believes phone calls should only happen during the daytime.
Heavy snow makes winter travel between two cities difficult.

scan.q: At what time did Ruth make the first snow call?

validation_note: 검증 완료: 본문만 근거로 다시 풀어 5문항+gist 정답이 유일함을 확인했습니다. 본문 183단어로 티어2 범위 내, scan.answer('at two in the morning')는 본문에 그대로 등장합니다. '정답=유일 최장' 문항은 0/6입니다. Q4의 '짐 싸던 날' 오답은 약속이 시작된 날(농담)과 실제 첫 전화를 구분해야 갈리는 함정으로, 본문을 읽어야만 정답이 유일하게 정해집니다. 수정 없음.

title: The Cure for the Afternoon Slump
text:
Many office workers feel sleepy after lunch and blame the meal itself. The real causes are a little different. After eating, the body sends extra blood to the stomach to help with digestion. At the same time, the body's inner clock naturally lowers energy in the early afternoon. Together, these two effects can make even a well-rested person struggle to stay focused at a desk.

Health experts suggest a simple response: a short walk. Walking for ten to twenty minutes after lunch helps the muscles take up sugar from the blood, so energy stays steadier through the afternoon. The gentle movement also raises the heart rate slightly, which makes people feel more awake. Coffee can help too, but a cup in the late afternoon may disturb sleep at night. A walk gives the same lift without that cost.

Making the walk a habit is easier with small tricks. Choose a regular route, invite a coworker, and go outside if possible, because daylight also helps the body clock stay on schedule. Even a few laps around the hallway are better than staying in a chair.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: How coffee slowly damages the quality of nighttime sleep
Why workers should replace lunch with a lighter meal
Why people get sleepy after lunch and how a walk helps
How the body digests sugar from different kinds of food
questions.explanation: 글 전체가 점심 후 졸림의 두 원인(소화를 위한 혈류 이동, 체내 시계)과 해결책인 짧은 산책을 다루므로 2번이 정답입니다. 나머지는 본문의 일부 소재만 확대했거나 다루지 않은 내용입니다.

questions.q: Why might a walk be a better afternoon choice than coffee, according to the passage?
options: It wakes people up without risking their sleep at night
It burns more calories than any other daily exercise
It costs less money than buying a cup of coffee every day
It works faster than coffee at raising energy levels
questions.explanation: 본문은 'a cup in the late afternoon may disturb sleep at night'라고 하면서 산책은 'the same lift without that cost'를 준다고 했으므로 0번이 맞습니다. 돈이나 속도 비교는 본문에 없습니다.

questions.q: What can we infer about daylight from the passage?
options: Daylight makes the stomach digest food more quickly
Daylight is harmful to workers in the early afternoon
Daylight matters less than the exercise for staying awake
Walking outside adds a benefit that indoor walking lacks
questions.explanation: 본문이 가능하면 밖으로 나가라며 'daylight also helps the body clock stay on schedule'이라고 했으므로, 밖에서 걷는 것이 실내 걷기에는 없는 추가 이점을 준다는 3번이 타당합니다.

questions.q: According to the passage, what are the two causes of the after-lunch energy dip?
options: Heavy lunch food and a lack of good sleep the night before
Blood moving to the stomach and the body's inner clock
Low blood sugar and too much time sitting in a chair
Warm office air and the sound of coworkers talking
questions.explanation: 본문은 소화를 돕기 위해 위로 가는 혈류와 'the body's inner clock'이 함께 작용한다고 명시했으므로 1번이 정답입니다. 잘 쉰 사람도 졸리다고 했으니 수면 부족은 원인이 아닙니다.

questions.q: How does walking keep energy steadier, according to the passage?
options: It helps the muscles take up sugar from the blood
It moves extra blood away from the brain and heart
It cools the body down after eating a warm meal
It slows digestion so the stomach works less hard
questions.explanation: 본문에 'helps the muscles take up sugar from the blood, so energy stays steadier'라고 명시되어 있으므로 0번이 정답입니다.

gist.q: Which sentence best states the main idea of the passage?
options: Drinking coffee is the most reliable way to stay awake at work.
A short walk after lunch is a simple fix for the afternoon dip.
Eating a smaller lunch is the best way to avoid feeling tired.
Afternoon tiredness mainly comes from a lack of sleep at night.

scan.q: How many minutes of walking after lunch does the passage suggest?

validation_note: 적대 검증 완료: 5문항+gist를 본문만으로 재풀이해 정답 유일성 확인. 단어수 184(티어2 140~200 내). '정답=유일 최장' 문항 0개. scan 답 'ten to twenty minutes' 본문 등장 확인. 보기 길이 편차 최대 16%로 균형. 자연스러운 원어민 영어. 수정 불필요.

title: The Trouble with Open Offices
text:
About twenty years ago, many companies began tearing down office walls. The new open floors had two selling points: workers could talk easily across the room, and one big space cost less to build than many small rooms. Managers hoped that easy conversation would lead to fresh ideas.

The results often went the other way. In open offices, phones ring, keyboards click, and every conversation can be heard by twenty people. To protect their focus, many workers put on headphones and answer questions by online message instead of turning to the person beside them. Studies that tracked office behavior found that face-to-face conversation actually fell after walls were removed, while written messages rose sharply.

Companies are now searching for a middle path. Some add quiet rooms where anyone can work alone for a few hours. Others set 'library hours,' fixed times when the whole floor stays silent. The lesson is not that open space is always bad. It is that office design should match the work: lively areas for teamwork, and protected corners for tasks that need deep attention.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Office noise problems can be fully solved by giving workers headphones
Open offices often failed, so companies now mix open and quiet space
Companies removed their office walls mainly to save money on rent
Written messages are a better way to share new ideas than talking
questions.explanation: 벽을 없앤 개방형 사무실이 기대와 반대 결과를 낳았고, 이제 회사들이 개방 공간과 조용한 공간을 섞는 중간 길을 찾는다는 흐름이 글의 중심이므로 1번이 정답입니다.

questions.q: What do the study results suggest about the managers' original hope?
options: It came true even faster than anyone at the companies expected
It was achieved, but only in the largest open offices
It mattered less to managers than the cost of building
It backfired, since removing walls cut spoken conversation
questions.explanation: 관리자들은 대화가 늘어 새로운 아이디어가 나오길 바랐지만, 연구 결과 'face-to-face conversation actually fell'이라고 했으므로 기대가 역효과를 냈다는 3번이 맞습니다.

questions.q: According to the passage, what were the two selling points of open floors?
options: Quieter rooms and better lighting for every worker
More privacy for workers and shorter meeting times
Easy conversation across the room and lower building costs
Faster online messages and extra space for new office equipment
questions.explanation: 본문은 'workers could talk easily across the room'과 큰 공간 하나가 작은 방 여러 개보다 짓는 비용이 적다는 점을 두 가지 장점으로 들었으므로 2번이 정답입니다.

questions.q: What is one solution some companies now use, according to the passage?
options: Fixed times when the whole floor stays silent
Paying workers to answer messages from home
Moving all phone calls to one shared meeting room
Hiring managers to watch conversations closely
questions.explanation: 본문에 'library hours,' fixed times when the whole floor stays silent'가 명시되어 있으므로 0번이 정답입니다. 나머지는 본문에 없는 방안입니다.

questions.q: Which task would most likely belong in a 'protected corner' of the office?
options: A team party to welcome a new group of workers
A quick chat about weekend plans with teammates
Careful checking of a long report for small errors
A loud brainstorming meeting for a new product idea
questions.explanation: 본문은 'protected corners for tasks that need deep attention'이라고 했으므로, 깊은 집중이 필요한 보고서 오류 검토인 2번이 가장 알맞습니다. 나머지는 활기찬 팀워크 공간에 어울리는 활동입니다.

gist.q: Which sentence best captures the passage's central point?
options: Open offices prove that workers talk more when walls come down.
Silence is the single most important condition for good work.
Companies should return to giving every worker a private room.
Good office design mixes open areas with quiet corners.

scan.q: What do many workers put on to protect their focus in open offices?

validation_note: 적대 검증 완료: 5문항+gist를 본문만으로 재풀이해 정답 유일성 확인. 단어수 179(티어2 140~200 내). '정답=유일 최장' 문항 0개. scan 답 'headphones' 본문 등장 확인. 보기 편차 최대 26%(Q2)이나 정답이 최장 보기가 아니라 단서 유출 없음. 자연스러운 원어민 영어. 수정 불필요.

title: The Real Price of Movie Popcorn
text:
Visitors to a movie theater often get a shock at the snack counter. A bag of popcorn that costs a few cents to make may sell for the price of a full meal. Many people call this unfair, but the high price follows a clear business logic.

The first reason is that theaters keep only part of the ticket money. During a film's first weeks, a large share of each ticket goes to the studio that made the movie. Snacks are different: the theater keeps almost everything it earns at the counter. The second reason is location. Once customers walk in, they cannot buy food from anywhere else, so the counter faces no competition inside the building.

Strangely, expensive popcorn may actually help moviegoers. Because snack profits pay so many of the bills, theaters can keep ticket prices lower than they would otherwise be. People who skip the snacks get a cheaper night out, paid for in part by those who buy the large popcorn. Seen this way, the price at the counter is less a trick than a quiet trade.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: The business reasons behind the high price of movie snacks
The history of popcorn as the favorite food of moviegoers
The ways studios decide which theaters may show their films
The fall in theater visits caused by high ticket prices
questions.explanation: 글 전체가 영화관 간식이 비싼 사업적 이유(티켓 수익 배분, 내부 경쟁 부재, 티켓 가격 보조)를 설명하므로 0번이 정답입니다.

questions.q: Based on the passage, what would likely happen if snack profits disappeared?
options: Studios would take a smaller share of each movie ticket
Theaters would start making their popcorn at a much lower cost
Theaters would probably have to raise their ticket prices
Customers would begin bringing their own food inside
questions.explanation: 본문은 'snack profits pay so many of the bills' 덕분에 극장이 티켓 가격을 낮게 유지한다고 했으므로, 그 이익이 사라지면 티켓 가격을 올려야 할 가능성이 큽니다. 2번이 맞습니다.

questions.q: Where does a large share of ticket money go during a film's first weeks?
options: To the workers who serve customers at the snack counter
To the studio that made the movie being shown
To the company that owns the theater building
To the city government as a special entertainment tax
questions.explanation: 본문에 'a large share of each ticket goes to the studio that made the movie'라고 명시되어 있으므로 1번이 정답입니다.

questions.q: According to the passage, why do people who skip snacks still benefit?
options: They can watch the movie from the best seats in the theater
They receive free drinks after buying several tickets
They avoid the long lines that form at the snack counter
They enjoy lower ticket prices supported by snack profits
questions.explanation: 본문은 간식을 사지 않는 사람도 'get a cheaper night out'라며, 간식 이익이 티켓 가격을 낮춰 준다고 했으므로 3번이 정답입니다.

questions.q: What does the phrase 'a quiet trade' suggest about popcorn prices?
options: Theaters secretly change their prices during each movie
Snack buyers help pay for the lower prices others enjoy
Customers are never told the real cost of making popcorn
Theaters and studios quietly agree on the price of snacks
questions.explanation: 본문은 팝콘 값이 속임수(trick)라기보다 조용한 거래라며, 큰 팝콘을 사는 사람들이 다른 사람의 싼 티켓 값을 일부 대신 낸다고 설명하므로 1번이 맞습니다.

gist.q: Which sentence best states the main idea of the passage?
options: Popcorn is cheap to make, so theaters should lower its price.
Studios earn most of their money from selling snacks in theaters.
High snack prices help theaters survive and keep tickets cheaper.
Moviegoers should always eat a full meal before going to a theater.

scan.q: According to the passage, how much does a bag of popcorn cost to make?

validation_note: 적대 검증 완료: 5문항+gist를 본문만으로 재풀이해 정답 유일성 확인. 단어수 181(티어2 140~200 내). '정답=유일 최장' 문항 0개. scan 답 'a few cents' 본문 등장 확인. Q4 오답3('원가를 알려주지 않는다')은 본문이 'a few cents'로 원가를 밝혀 소거됨. 보기 편차 최대 22%(Q2)이나 정답이 최장 보기 아님. 자연스러운 원어민 영어. 수정 불필요.

title: The Case for Getting Lost
text:
Modern travel promises a kind of frictionless efficiency. With a phone in hand, a visitor can be guided turn by turn to a famous square, shown the fastest route, and warned about every wrong turn before it happens. Nothing is left to chance, and almost nothing is left to discovery. Yet many seasoned travelers quietly admit that their sharpest memories come from the moments when the plan fell apart.

There is a reason for this. When we follow a fixed route, our attention narrows to the screen and the destination, and the streets in between blur into mere distance to be crossed. When we lose our way, the opposite happens: the brain switches into a more watchful mode, reading signs, faces, and smells for clues. A wrong turn forces a conversation with a stranger, an unplanned café, a building no guidebook mentions. The city stops being a checklist and becomes a place.

This does not mean throwing away every map. It means leaving room for the unplanned — setting aside an afternoon with no schedule, choosing a street simply because it looks interesting. The reward is not efficiency but a richer sense of having actually been somewhere. In travel, as in much of life, the detour is often where the journey truly begins.

questions.q: What is the main point the passage makes about travel?
options: Travelers should throw away their maps and phones on every trip.
Making room for the unplanned can enrich a trip more than efficiency.
The fastest turn-by-turn route to a landmark is almost always the best one.
Visiting the most famous squares is the single true purpose of any journey.
questions.explanation: 본문은 지도를 완전히 버리라는 게 아니라 '계획하지 않은 것을 위한 여지를 남기라'며 효율보다 더 풍부한 여행 경험을 강조합니다. 따라서 정답은 1번입니다.

questions.q: According to the passage, what happens to a traveler's attention when they follow a fixed route?
options: It sharpens, helping them notice more details along the way.
It shifts entirely onto the people they are traveling with.
It narrows so that the streets in between barely register.
It becomes focused on planning the next day's schedule.
questions.explanation: 본문은 고정된 경로를 따르면 '주의가 화면과 목적지로 좁아지고 그 사이 거리는 흐릿해진다'고 합니다. 따라서 정답은 2번입니다.

questions.q: Why do many seasoned travelers say their sharpest memories come from when the plan fell apart?
options: Because a plan that falls apart makes travelers cut the trip short and return home.
Because getting lost forces richer, unplanned encounters with a place.
Because seasoned travelers secretly dislike the famous squares in every guidebook.
Because the fast, efficient routes they follow are usually blocked by heavy traffic.
questions.explanation: 본문은 길을 잃으면 뇌가 더 경계 태세가 되어 낯선 이와의 대화, 예정에 없던 카페 등 풍부한 만남이 생긴다고 합니다. 가장 선명한 기억이 계획이 틀어진 순간에서 온다는 근거이므로 정답은 1번입니다.

questions.q: According to the passage, what does a wrong turn force on a traveler?
options: A long, frustrating wait for the next available public transport.
A quick return to the fast route the phone first recommended.
A conversation with a stranger and a stop at an unplanned café.
A rushed change to the crowded schedule planned for the next day.
questions.explanation: 본문은 잘못된 방향 전환이 '낯선 이와의 대화, 예정에 없던 카페(a conversation with a stranger, an unplanned café)'를 만들어낸다고 명시합니다. 따라서 정답은 2번입니다.

questions.q: What does the phrase 'the detour is often where the journey truly begins' suggest?
options: Real discovery often happens in a trip's unplanned parts.
Every journey should be started with one deliberately long detour.
Detours simply waste the limited time a traveler has for a trip.
The opening stage of a trip is always its least important part.
questions.explanation: '우회로가 여정이 진짜 시작되는 곳'이라는 표현은 계획하지 않은 부분에서 진짜 발견이 일어난다는 뜻입니다. 따라서 정답은 0번입니다.

gist.q: Which sentence best captures the central idea of the passage?
options: Minute-by-minute planning is what makes travel enjoyable and stress-free.
Leaving space for the unplanned makes a journey richer than pure efficiency.
Turn-by-turn maps and phones have quietly ruined travel for everyone.
Only the most famous landmarks are ever truly worth visiting on a trip.

scan.q: According to the passage, what does the city become when it stops being a checklist?

validation_note: 적대적 재검증 완료. 단어수 212(170~240 B2 범위 내). 전 5문항·gist·scan 정답을 본문 근거로 대조해 유일함 확인. 보기 길이 재조정 후 '정답=유일 최장' 문항 0개, 문항별 길이 편차 최대 20%(±25% 이내). Q3(잘못된 방향 전환) 정답 인덱스를 3→2로 옮기며 answer·explanation 동기화. scan 'a place'는 본문 등장 확인.

title: What Keeps a Friendship Alive
text:
We tend to picture friendship as something built on grand moments — a dramatic rescue in a crisis, a heartfelt confession at midnight, a loyalty tested and proven. These stories are satisfying, but they describe the exception rather than the rule. Most friendships are held together by something far quieter: a steady stream of small, almost forgettable exchanges.

Consider how friendships usually end. It is rarely a single explosive argument. More often, two people simply stop sending the quick message, stop remembering the small detail, stop showing up for the ordinary Tuesday. Nothing is broken on purpose; the connection just thins until one day it is gone. What sustains a bond, then, is not the size of each gesture but the regularity of it.

This reframes what it means to be a good friend. It is less about being available for the rare emergency and more about the unglamorous habit of staying in touch. A short note that says 'I saw this and thought of you' can carry more weight over the years than a single grand act of devotion. Friendship, in this light, resembles a savings account: it grows not through occasional windfalls but through modest, repeated deposits that quietly compound into something strong.

questions.q: What is the passage's main argument about friendship?
options: Friendships depend mainly on surviving one dramatic, midnight test of loyalty.
Deep midnight conversations are the only thing that truly matters in friendship.
Friendships are sustained more by small, regular contact than grand gestures.
Most friendships end abruptly because of a single explosive argument one night.
questions.explanation: 본문 전체는 우정이 극적 순간이 아니라 '작고 규칙적인 접촉'으로 유지된다고 주장합니다. 따라서 정답은 2번입니다.

questions.q: According to the passage, how do friendships usually end?
options: Through the slow thinning of small, routine contact.
Through one dramatic argument that neither person forgets.
Because one friend suddenly moves to a distant city.
Because a promise of loyalty is broken in a crisis.
questions.explanation: 본문은 우정이 대개 폭발적 다툼이 아니라 작은 접촉이 서서히 사라지며 끝난다고 합니다. 따라서 정답은 0번입니다.

questions.q: What does comparing friendship to a savings account suggest?
options: That friendships should be treated as a financial investment.
That only wealthy people can maintain close friendships.
That grand, occasional gestures matter most for a bond.
That small, repeated efforts build a strong bond.
questions.explanation: 저축 계좌 비유는 가끔의 횡재가 아니라 '작고 반복적인 예치'가 쌓여 강해진다는 뜻입니다. 따라서 정답은 3번입니다.

questions.q: What example does the passage give of a small but meaningful gesture?
options: Rescuing a friend during a dangerous, life-threatening crisis.
A short note saying 'I saw this and thought of you.'
A heartfelt confession shared quietly at midnight.
Proving one's loyalty through a long and difficult test.
questions.explanation: 본문은 작지만 의미 있는 몸짓의 예로 '이걸 보고 네 생각이 났어'라는 짧은 쪽지를 듭니다. 따라서 정답은 1번입니다.

questions.q: What does the passage imply about being a good friend?
options: Staying in regular touch matters more than rare heroics.
One must wait for a crisis to prove real devotion.
Ordinary, everyday interactions are best avoided entirely.
A single unforgettable gesture defines a true friend.
questions.explanation: 본문은 좋은 친구란 드문 응급 상황보다 '꾸준히 연락을 유지하는 습관'에 가깝다고 합니다. 따라서 정답은 0번입니다.

gist.q: Which statement best summarizes the passage?
options: Friendship survives through small, regular contact more than grand gestures.
A true friendship requires surviving at least one major crisis together to last.
Friendships almost always end suddenly after a single serious falling-out at night.
Only deep, lengthy midnight conversations can keep a real friendship alive.

scan.q: According to the passage, what does the writer compare friendship to?

validation_note: 적대적 재검증 완료. 단어수 204(B2 범위 내). 전 5문항·gist·scan 정답을 본문 근거로 대조해 유일함 확인. 보기 길이 재조정 후 '정답=유일 최장' 문항 0개, 문항별 길이 편차 최대 24%(±25% 이내). scan 'a savings account'는 본문 등장 확인.

title: Why We Eat Together
text:
Eating is a biological necessity, but eating together is something more. In nearly every culture, important moments — a wedding, a holiday, a peace agreement — are marked by a shared meal. This is no accident. Long before humans had written contracts, sitting down to divide food was a way of signaling trust: I will share what I have with you, and expect you to do the same.

Researchers who study cooperation notice that people who eat together tend to work together more smoothly afterward. The reasons are partly practical and partly symbolic. Passing a dish, waiting for others to be served, matching one another's pace — these small acts require the same coordination that any group effort demands. A meal is, in a sense, a low-stakes rehearsal for getting along.

Modern life has quietly chipped away at this ritual. More meals are now eaten alone, at a desk or in front of a screen, treated as fuel to be consumed as fast as possible. The food may be just as nourishing, yet something else goes missing. When we stop sharing the table, we lose one of the oldest and simplest tools our species has for turning a group of separate individuals into something that feels like a community.

questions.q: What is the central idea of the passage?
options: Eating alone at a desk is far more nutritious than eating with others.
Sharing meals does more than feed people; it builds trust and cooperation.
Ancient humans invented written contracts long before they ever shared food.
Weddings and holidays are the only occasions when people should eat together.
questions.explanation: 본문은 함께 먹는 일이 영양 이상으로 신뢰와 협력을 쌓는다고 말합니다. 따라서 정답은 1번입니다.

questions.q: According to the passage, what did dividing food signal before written contracts existed?
options: A willingness to share what one had and to trust one another.
That one group had just defeated another group in battle.
A practical way of preserving food for the long winter months.
That an important wedding or holiday was about to begin nearby.
questions.explanation: 본문은 문서 계약 이전에 음식을 나누는 것이 '내 것을 나누고 너도 그러길 기대한다'는 신뢰의 신호였다고 합니다. 따라서 정답은 0번입니다.

questions.q: Why does the passage call a meal 'a low-stakes rehearsal for getting along'?
options: Because a shared meal is much cheaper than most other group activities.
Because eating a meal together requires no real cooperation at all.
Because the coordination it needs mirrors that of any group effort.
Because people almost never argue with each other while they are eating.
questions.explanation: 본문은 음식을 건네고 차례를 기다리며 속도를 맞추는 일이 어떤 집단 활동에도 필요한 협응과 같다고 설명합니다. 따라서 정답은 2번입니다.

questions.q: According to the passage, how are more meals eaten in modern life?
options: Slowly, spread across long conversations with family.
Alone, at a desk or in front of a screen, treated as fuel.
Outdoors, at large gatherings of the whole community.
Only during major weddings and religious holidays.
questions.explanation: 본문은 현대에 더 많은 식사가 '책상에서나 화면 앞에서 혼자(alone, at a desk or in front of a screen)' 이뤄진다고 합니다. 따라서 정답은 1번입니다.

questions.q: What does the passage suggest is lost when people stop sharing meals?
options: The nutritional value of the food that they actually eat.
The ability to prepare their food both quickly and cheaply.
A simple, ancient tool for building a sense of community.
Any remaining interest in celebrating weddings or holidays.
questions.explanation: 본문은 식탁을 나누길 멈추면 개인들을 공동체로 만드는 '가장 오래되고 단순한 도구(one of the oldest and simplest tools)'를 잃는다고 합니다. 영양가는 그대로라고 명시하므로 0번은 오답입니다. 따라서 정답은 2번입니다.

gist.q: Which sentence best captures the central idea of the passage?
options: Fast, solo eating at a screen is the healthiest modern habit.
Shared meals build trust and community, not just nutrition.
Written contracts long ago replaced any need to share food.
People should only ever eat together on rare special occasions.

scan.q: According to the passage, what does the writer call a meal, in terms of learning to get along?

validation_note: 적대적 재검증 완료. 단어수 207(B2 범위 내). 전 5문항·gist·scan 정답을 본문 근거로 대조해 유일함 확인. 보기 길이 재조정 후 '정답=유일 최장' 문항은 Q3(현대 식사 방식) 1개뿐(편당 1개 한도 이내), 문항별 길이 편차 최대 16%. Q4(잃는 것) 정답 인덱스를 3→2로 옮기며 answer·explanation 동기화. scan 'a low-stakes rehearsal'는 본문 등장 확인.

title: Why Stores Love Coupons
text:
A coupon looks like a small gift from a store to its customers. In reality, it is a classic tool of a strategy economists call price discrimination: charging different customers different amounts for the same product. Ideally, a store would charge every shopper the maximum that person is willing to pay. The trouble is that stores cannot read minds. Coupons solve this problem in an elegant way — they let shoppers sort themselves.

The mechanism depends on effort. Finding, clipping, or downloading coupons takes time, and only some people consider that time well spent. Shoppers who care deeply about price accept the small hassle and pay less. Shoppers who value their time more highly skip the search and pay full price. Without asking a single question, the store has divided its customers into two groups and charged each one close to what it will tolerate.

This logic also explains why coupons are often mildly inconvenient on purpose. If the discount were applied automatically at the register, everyone would receive it, including the customers who were perfectly happy to pay more, and the store would simply earn less. The inconvenience is not a design flaw; it is the entire point. Each coupon quietly asks a question: how much is your time actually worth?

questions.q: What is the main point of the passage?
options: Coupons are gifts stores use to thank their most loyal and frequent customers.
Stores lose money whenever they allow shoppers to use downloaded coupons.
Coupons let stores charge different prices by making shoppers sort themselves.
Shoppers should always search for coupons because the savings outweigh the time.
questions.explanation: 본문은 쿠폰이 'price discrimination'의 도구이며 'they let shoppers sort themselves(쇼핑객이 스스로를 분류하게 한다)'라고 설명합니다. 따라서 정답은 2번입니다. 0번은 첫 문장에서 '선물처럼 보일 뿐'이라고 부정되고, 1번과 3번은 본문의 주장이 아닙니다.

questions.q: Why does the passage say that stores 'cannot read minds'?
options: They have no direct way of knowing the highest price each shopper would accept.
They are unable to predict which products customers will place in their carts.
They cannot tell whether a coupon was clipped from paper or downloaded online.
They never find out how many of their coupons are actually used at the register.
questions.explanation: 바로 앞 문장이 '모든 쇼핑객에게 그 사람이 지불할 의사가 있는 최대 금액을 받고 싶다(the maximum that person is willing to pay)'는 이상을 말하고, 그 장애물로 '마음을 읽을 수 없다'가 나옵니다. 각자의 최대 지불 의사를 알 수 없다는 뜻이므로 정답은 0번입니다.

questions.q: According to the passage, what do shoppers who value their time highly tend to do?
options: They download coupons in advance to avoid delays at the register.
They complain to the store about how inconvenient the coupons are.
They wait until the store applies its discounts automatically.
They skip the coupon search entirely and pay the full price.
questions.explanation: 본문은 '시간을 더 높게 치는 쇼핑객은 검색을 건너뛰고 정가를 낸다(skip the search and pay full price)'고 명시합니다. 따라서 정답은 3번입니다.

questions.q: What can be inferred about a store that made all of its discounts automatic at the register?
options: It would attract many new customers and increase its overall earnings.
It would give up money from customers who were willing to pay more.
It would need to print far more paper coupons than it did before.
It would divide its shoppers into two groups more effectively than coupons do.
questions.explanation: 본문은 할인이 자동 적용되면 '기꺼이 더 낼 손님까지 모두 할인을 받아 가게는 그만큼 덜 벌게 된다(the store would simply earn less)'고 합니다. 따라서 정답은 1번입니다.

questions.q: According to the passage, what does the coupon mechanism depend on?
options: The effort required to find and use the coupons.
The friendliness of the staff at the register.
The number of stores competing in the same area.
The quality of the products being discounted.
questions.explanation: 둘째 문단 첫 문장이 'The mechanism depends on effort(이 장치는 수고에 달려 있다)'라고 직접 말합니다. 따라서 정답은 0번입니다.

gist.q: Which sentence best captures the central idea of the passage?
options: Paper coupons are disappearing as more stores move their discounts online.
Most shoppers greatly overestimate how much money coupons actually save them each year.
Stores design coupons to reward the customers who spend the most each year.
Coupons deliberately use inconvenience to charge different shoppers different prices.

scan.q: What do economists call the strategy of charging different customers different amounts for the same product?

validation_note: 적대 검증 통과: 본문 212단어로 티어3(B2) 범위(170~240) 충족. 5문항+요지를 본문만으로 재풀이해 정답 유일성 확인(정답 인덱스 2,0,3,1,0,3으로 분산). 보기 길이 편차 전부 ±25% 이내, '정답이 유일하게 최장'인 문항 0개. scan.answer 'price discrimination'은 본문 원문 부분열. 오답은 모두 본문 소재(다운로드 쿠폰·정가·자동 할인 등)를 활용한 틀린 진술이라 상식만으로 소거 불가.

title: Making Bad Habits Harder
text:
Most advice about breaking bad habits centers on willpower: decide firmly, resist temptation, and try again after every failure. Researchers who study behavior, however, keep arriving at a less heroic conclusion. The most reliable way to change a habit is not to fight it directly but to redesign the environment around it, because habits survive on convenience.

Consider a few small experiments. People who moved their phone to another room in the evening reported checking it far less, even though walking to get it took only about twenty seconds. Snacks placed in opaque containers on a high shelf were eaten less often than snacks left visible on the counter. Deleting a social media app, so that using it required logging in through a browser, cut usage sharply. None of these obstacles is difficult to overcome. They work because habits run almost automatically, and even a brief delay gives the conscious mind a chance to interrupt and ask whether the action is truly wanted.

The principle also works in reverse. Placing running shoes beside the door or a water bottle on the desk removes friction from good habits. The broader lesson is to shape the path instead of battling yourself: willpower rises and falls with mood and fatigue, but a well-arranged environment keeps working all day.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Bad habits can only be broken by people with unusually strong willpower.
Changing the environment around a habit works better than fighting it directly.
Social media apps are the hardest habits for most people to bring under control.
Good habits form naturally once all bad habits have been removed first.
questions.explanation: 첫 문단이 '습관을 바꾸는 가장 믿을 만한 방법은 정면으로 싸우는 것이 아니라 주변 환경을 다시 설계하는 것(to redesign the environment around it)'이라고 못 박고, 이후 전체가 그 근거입니다. 따라서 정답은 1번입니다.

questions.q: According to the passage's reasoning, why does a delay of only twenty seconds reduce phone checking?
options: Twenty seconds is long enough for the phone's screen to lock itself again.
People forget what they wanted to look at during the short walk to the phone.
Walking to another room uses up the energy people would spend on the phone.
The pause gives the conscious mind a moment to question the automatic urge.
questions.explanation: 본문은 '습관은 거의 자동으로 돌아가고, 짧은 지연조차 의식적인 정신이 끼어들어 정말 원하는 행동인지 물을 기회를 준다(a brief delay gives the conscious mind a chance to interrupt)'고 설명합니다. 따라서 정답은 3번입니다.

questions.q: What does the phrase 'a less heroic conclusion' suggest about the researchers' approach?
options: It does not rely on exceptional personal strength or dramatic effort.
It was discovered by ordinary people rather than by famous scientists.
It is considered less impressive and therefore receives little funding.
It only works for people who have already failed many times before.
questions.explanation: '영웅적(heroic)'인 첫 문장의 의지력 조언(굳게 결심하고 유혹에 저항하라)과 대비되는 표현입니다. 특별한 정신력 없이도 되는 방법이라는 뜻이므로 정답은 0번입니다.

questions.q: According to the passage, what happened to snacks stored in opaque containers on a high shelf?
options: They were eaten more quickly because people saw them as special treats.
They were forgotten completely and eventually had to be thrown away.
They were eaten less often than snacks left visible on the counter.
They were replaced by healthier foods that people kept on the desk.
questions.explanation: 본문은 '불투명한 용기에 담아 높은 선반에 둔 간식은 조리대에 보이게 둔 간식보다 덜 먹혔다(were eaten less often)'고 명시합니다. 따라서 정답은 2번입니다.

questions.q: Which of the following is given as an example of removing friction from a good habit?
options: Deleting a social media app from the phone.
Placing running shoes beside the door.
Storing snacks inside opaque containers.
Moving the phone to another room at night.
questions.explanation: 셋째 문단에서 '문 옆에 운동화를 두는 것(placing running shoes beside the door)'을 좋은 습관의 마찰을 줄이는 예로 듭니다. 나머지 셋은 반대로 나쁜 습관에 장애물을 더하는 예이므로 정답은 1번입니다.

gist.q: Which sentence best summarizes the passage?
options: Willpower grows stronger every time a person resists a temptation.
People check their phones less when they feel rested and calm.
Small changes to surroundings can break bad habits and support good ones.
Habits are impossible to change once they have become automatic.

scan.q: About how many seconds did it take to walk to a phone left in another room?

validation_note: 적대 검증 통과: 본문 215단어로 범위(170~240) 충족. 전 문항을 본문 근거로 재풀이해 정답 유일성 확인(정답 인덱스 1,3,0,2,1,2로 분산). 보기 길이 편차 전부 ±25% 이내이고 '정답이 유일하게 최장'인 문항은 요지 1개뿐(6문항 중 1개로 1/3 이하). 5번의 오답 3개는 모두 본문 속 실제 사례(반대 방향 예시)라 본문을 읽어야만 소거됩니다. scan.answer 'about twenty seconds'는 본문 원문 부분열.

title: How the Weekend Was Invented
text:
The two-day weekend feels as natural as sunrise, yet it is barely a century old. For most of recorded history, workers rested one day at most, and that day was set by religious custom rather than by any idea of leisure. In nineteenth-century Britain, factory workers created an unofficial holiday of their own, skipping work on Mondays to recover from Sunday amusements. The custom became so common that it earned a nickname, 'Saint Monday,' and employers came to despise its unpredictability.

The solution was a bargain. Many factories offered workers a free Saturday afternoon in exchange for reliable attendance on Monday morning. In the United States, another pressure pushed in the same direction: some factories employed both Jewish workers, whose day of rest was Saturday, and Christian workers, who rested on Sunday. Giving everyone both days made scheduling far simpler. By the 1920s, several large manufacturers had adopted a five-day week, partly reasoning that employees with free time would become customers with time to spend.

Laws and union contracts gradually fixed the pattern in place during the following decades. The history is worth remembering because it shows that even rhythms of life that feel timeless are products of negotiation, and today's debates about a four-day week suggest the bargaining is not finished.

questions.q: What is the main point of the passage?
options: The weekend is a recent arrangement produced by bargaining, religion, and economics.
Workers in nineteenth-century Britain enjoyed more holidays than workers do today.
Religious rules are the only reason people ever stopped working on certain days.
The five-day week failed because employers found it too expensive to maintain.
questions.explanation: 본문은 주말이 '겨우 한 세기밖에 안 된' 발명품이며 종교 관습, 노사 흥정, 제조업체의 경제적 계산이 겹쳐 만들어졌고 '자연스러워 보이는 리듬도 협상의 산물(products of negotiation)'이라고 결론짓습니다. 따라서 정답은 0번입니다.

questions.q: According to the passage, what was 'Saint Monday'?
options: A religious festival that factories celebrated at the start of each month.
A bonus payment given to workers who arrived reliably on Monday mornings.
An unofficial custom of skipping Monday work to recover from Sunday amusements.
A law that required all British factories to close on the first day of each week.
questions.explanation: 본문은 노동자들이 '일요일의 유흥에서 회복하려고 월요일에 일을 빼먹는(skipping work on Mondays)' 비공식 관습에 'Saint Monday'라는 별명이 붙었다고 설명합니다. 따라서 정답은 2번입니다.

questions.q: Why did factories offer workers a free Saturday afternoon?
options: They wanted to give workers time to attend religious services on Saturday.
They hoped the deal would make workers show up dependably on Mondays.
They needed the afternoon to repair machines that ran all week long.
They believed shorter weeks would let them hire fewer workers overall.
questions.explanation: 본문은 토요일 오후 휴무가 '월요일 아침의 확실한 출근과 맞바꾼(in exchange for reliable attendance on Monday morning)' 거래였다고 하며, 그 배경은 고용주가 싫어한 'Saint Monday'의 예측 불가능성입니다. 따라서 정답은 1번입니다.

questions.q: According to the passage, why did employing both Jewish and Christian workers matter?
options: It forced factories to stay open seven days a week to serve both groups.
It caused disputes that unions eventually had to settle through contracts.
It meant factories needed two separate buildings for the two communities.
It encouraged giving everyone both rest days, which simplified scheduling.
questions.explanation: 본문은 안식일이 각각 토요일과 일요일인 두 집단이 함께 일했기에 '모두에게 이틀을 다 주는 것이 일정 관리를 훨씬 단순하게 만들었다(Giving everyone both days made scheduling far simpler)'고 명시합니다. 따라서 정답은 3번입니다.

questions.q: What does the mention of the four-day week at the end suggest?
options: Most countries have already passed laws that create a mandatory four-day week.
Workers today are less interested in leisure than earlier generations were.
The five-day week was officially abandoned during recent decades.
The shape of the working week may still be renegotiated in the future.
questions.explanation: 마지막 문장은 주4일제 논쟁이 '흥정이 아직 끝나지 않았음을 시사한다(the bargaining is not finished)'고 합니다. 즉 근무 주간의 형태가 앞으로도 다시 협상될 수 있다는 뜻이므로 정답은 3번입니다.

gist.q: Which sentence best captures the central idea of the passage?
options: The two-day weekend was invented through negotiation and could change again.
Employers created the weekend mainly as a reward for their most loyal factory workers.
Sunday rest has always mattered more to workers than Saturday rest ever did.
Factory work in the nineteenth century was much easier than office work today.

scan.q: What nickname was given to the workers' unofficial Monday holiday in nineteenth-century Britain?

validation_note: 적대 검증 통과: 본문 212단어로 범위(170~240) 충족. 실존 기업·인물 실명 없이 일반 명사로만 서술. 전 문항의 근거 문장을 본문에서 재확인해 정답 유일성 확인(정답 인덱스 0,2,1,3,3,0으로 분산). 보기 길이 편차 전부 ±25% 이내이고 '정답이 유일하게 최장'인 문항은 1번뿐(6문항 중 1개로 1/3 이하). scan.answer 'Saint Monday'는 본문 원문 부분열. 오답은 모두 본문 소재(종교 축일·보너스·노조 계약·건물 분리 등)를 활용한 틀린 진술이라 본문 없이는 소거 불가.

title: 公共汽车上的座位
text:
星期一早上，小美坐公共汽车去上班。车上人很多，她只找到一个站着的地方。这时，她看见一位老人也上了车，手里还拿着很多东西，站不太稳。小美马上站起来，把自己的座位让给了老人。老人很高兴，谢了她好几次。

小美站在老人旁边，扶着车上的把手。虽然要站二十分钟才能到公司，但是她心里觉得很开心。到站以后，老人下车前又对她笑了笑。小美觉得，这样的小事虽然简单，却能让一天都变得美好。

questions.q: 小美在车上做了什么？
options: 把座位让给了老人
跟旁边的老人聊天
帮老人拿东西下车
给老人指路
questions.explanation: 본문에 '자신의 자리를 노인에게 양보했다(把自己的座位让给了老人)'고 명시되어 있으므로 0번이 정답입니다. 노인과 이야기를 나누거나 짐을 들어 내려주거나 길을 알려준 내용은 본문에 없습니다.

questions.q: 老人上车的时候，为什么站不太稳？
options: 因为车开得太快，突然停下来吓到了他
因为他年纪大，手里还拿着很多东西
因为他一直找不到自己的座位
因为车上没有可以扶的把手
questions.explanation: 본문에서 노인이 '나이가 많고 손에 많은 물건을 들고 있어 잘 서 있지 못했다(老人也上了车，手里还拿着很多东西，站不太稳)'고 했으므로 1번이 정답입니다. 차가 급정거했다거나 손잡이가 없다는 내용은 본문에 근거가 없습니다.

questions.q: 小美站着到公司要多长时间？
options: 十分钟
二十分钟
半个小时
一个小时
questions.explanation: 본문에 '20분을 서 있어야 회사에 도착한다(要站二十分钟才能到公司)'고 나와 있으므로 1번이 정답입니다.

questions.q: 老人下车前对小美笑了笑，这说明了什么？
options: 老人觉得小美看起来很面熟
老人在感谢小美，心里很感激
老人想和小美一起下车回家
老人不想再坐这辆车了
questions.explanation: 노인은 이미 여러 번 감사 인사를 했고(谢了她好几次), 내리기 전 미소도 같은 맥락의 고마움 표현이므로 1번이 정답입니다. 다른 선택지는 본문에 근거가 없습니다.

questions.q: 从最后一句可以看出，小美是一个什么样的人？
options: 觉得给人让座是很麻烦的事
认为小事也能带来快乐的人
不太喜欢坐公共汽车的人
一个经常上班迟到的人
questions.explanation: 마지막 문장에서 '이런 작은 일도 하루를 아름답게 만들 수 있다고 느꼈다(这样的小事虽然简单，却能让一天都变得美好)'고 했으므로, 사소한 일에서도 기쁨을 찾는 사람이라는 1번이 정답입니다. 양보를 귀찮아하는 0번은 본문의 태도와 정반대입니다.

gist.q: 这段话主要讲的是什么？
options: 小美在公共汽车上给老人让座的事

title: 堵车的星期五
text:
星期五下午，爸爸开车去接小杰放学。平时只要十五分钟的路，那天却堵了很久。路上的车一辆接一辆，几乎不动。小杰坐在后面，看着窗外，问爸爸为什么今天车这么多。

爸爸说，星期五大家都急着回家，还有一些人要出去旅行，所以路上特别堵。他们等了差不多四十分钟，才终于到家。小杰说，以后星期五还是坐公共汽车比较好，因为公共汽车有自己的车道，不容易堵车。爸爸觉得这个办法不错，笑着答应了他。

questions.q: 爸爸那天开车接小杰，一共用了多长时间？
options: 平时要走的十五分钟
跟平时一样二十分钟
差不多四十分钟
比平时快的一个小时
questions.explanation: 본문에 '거의 40분을 기다려서야 집에 도착했다(等了差不多四十分钟，才终于到家)'고 나와 있으므로 2번이 정답입니다. 15분은 평소 걸리는 시간이고, 20분·1시간은 본문에 없습니다.

questions.q: 那天路上为什么车特别多？
options: 因为那条路正在修理，很不好走
因为下大雨，车都开得很慢
因为很多人星期五急着回家或去旅行
因为学校今天提前放学了
questions.explanation: 본문에서 아빠가 '금요일이라 다들 서둘러 귀가하고, 여행 떠나는 사람도 있어서(星期五大家都急着回家，还有一些人要出去旅行)'라고 설명했으므로 2번이 정답입니다. 도로 공사·비·조기 하교는 본문에 없습니다.

questions.q: 小杰觉得以后星期五应该怎么去接他？
options: 坐公共汽车
自己骑自行车
走路慢慢回家
坐地铁回家去
questions.explanation: 본문에 소걸이 '금요일에는 버스를 타는 게 낫겠다(还是坐公共汽车比较好)'고 말했으므로 0번이 정답입니다. 자전거·도보·지하철은 언급되지 않았습니다.

questions.q: 小杰认为坐公共汽车比开车好的原因是什么？
options: 因为公共汽车的车票很便宜
公共汽车有自己的车道，不容易堵车
因为公共汽车上有空调很凉快
因为公共汽车可以直接开到家门口
questions.explanation: 본문에 '버스는 전용 차로가 있어서 잘 막히지 않기 때문(因为公共汽车有自己的车道，不容易堵车)'이라고 이유가 명시되어 있으므로 1번이 정답입니다. 요금·에어컨·집 앞 도착은 본문에 근거가 없습니다.

questions.q: 爸爸对小杰的建议是什么态度？
options: 觉得不太可能，就拒绝了他
觉得这个办法不错，同意了
一直没有回答小杰的问题
让小杰自己去决定这件事
questions.explanation: 마지막 문장에서 아빠가 '이 방법이 괜찮다고 느끼며 웃으며 답했다(觉得这个办法不错，笑着答应了他)'고 했으므로 1번이 정답입니다.

gist.q: 这段话主要讲的是什么？
options: 一个星期五堵车，父子俩讨论以后怎么上学
小杰那天在学校里跟同学发生的一件事情

scan.q: 爸爸和小杰那天等了差不多多长时间才到家？

validation_note: 본문만으로 5문항+gist 재풀이해 정답 유일성 재확인. 한자수 164자(티어1 130~180 범위 내). scan answer '差不多四十分钟' 본문 등장 확인. 초기 검증에서 발견한 결함: 보기 길이 편향(문항 5개 전부 정답이 유일 최장, gist도 유일 최장 → 최장 선택만으로 만점 가능) → 오답 보기를 자연스럽게 보강해 재조정. 결과: 문항 중 유일-최장-정답 1개(Q2, 규칙 상한 이하), gist 유일 최장 아님(정답 18자, 오답도 18자 동률). 오답 보기는 본문에 근거 없음. 기존 zh3-02(共享单车·티어3)와 교통 소재 겹치나 티어·사건·주제분류 달라 중복 아님.

title: 秋天的第一场雨
text:
九月的一天，天气突然变凉了，下起了秋天的第一场雨。小云走在放学回家的路上，看见路边的树叶已经开始变黄，一片一片地掉在地上。她觉得又冷又有点想家，加快了脚步。

到家以后，妈妈正在厨房做热汤，屋子里很暖和。妈妈说，天气凉了，晚上睡觉要多盖一条被子，早上出门也要多穿一件衣服。小云喝了一碗热汤，身上慢慢暖和起来。她看着窗外的雨，觉得秋天虽然有点冷，但是家里总是很温暖。

questions.q: 小云放学回家的路上，看见了什么？
options: 路边突然下起了一场很大的雪
树叶变黄，一片一片地掉下来
路上有工人在忙着修路
有人在路边卖热汤
questions.explanation: 본문에 '길가의 나뭇잎이 이미 노랗게 변하기 시작해 한 잎 한 잎 떨어지고 있었다(路边的树叶已经开始变黄，一片一片地掉在地上)'고 나와 있으므로 1번이 정답입니다. 눈·도로 공사·국을 파는 사람은 본문에 없습니다.

questions.q: 小云在回家的路上为什么加快了脚步？
options: 因为她要去前面那家商店里买点东西
因为天冷了，她又想家又想快点到家
因为她怕赶不上公共汽车
因为路上人太多，走得很慢
questions.explanation: 본문에서 '춥고 조금 집이 그리워져서 발걸음을 재촉했다(又冷又有点想家，加快了脚步)'고 했으므로 1번이 정답입니다. 물건 사기·버스·혼잡은 본문에 근거가 없습니다.

questions.q: 小云到家以后，妈妈在做什么？
options: 在厨房里做热汤
在客厅里看电视
在院子里扫树叶
在房间里睡觉
questions.explanation: 본문에 '엄마가 부엌에서 뜨거운 국을 끓이고 있었다(妈妈正在厨房做热汤)'고 나와 있으므로 0번이 정답입니다. 나머지는 본문에 없습니다.

questions.q: 妈妈让小云晚上多盖被子、早上多穿衣服，这说明了什么？
options: 妈妈觉得小云已经长大了
妈妈很关心小云，怕她着凉
妈妈想让小云早一点去睡觉
妈妈不太喜欢下雨的天气
questions.explanation: 날씨가 추워졌으니 감기 걸리지 않게 이불과 옷을 더 챙기라고 당부한 것(天气凉了…要多盖…多穿)은 딸을 향한 세심한 걱정이므로 1번이 정답입니다.

questions.q: 从最后一句可以看出，小云对秋天的感觉是什么？
options: 觉得秋天实在是太冷了，很讨厌
觉得秋天有点冷，但家里很温暖
觉得秋天和夏天没有什么区别
觉得秋天更应该待在学校里
questions.explanation: 마지막 문장에서 '가을이 조금 춥지만 집은 늘 따뜻하다고 느꼈다(秋天虽然有点冷，但是家里总是很温暖)'고 했으므로 1번이 정답입니다. 너무 싫다(0번)는 본문의 따뜻한 결말과 어긋납니다.

gist.q: 这段话主要讲的是什么？
options: 小云在秋天第一场雨的傍晚回家，感受到家的温暖
小云那天放学以后在学校里认真上课又做功课的事
怎么在天冷的时候用简单的方法做一碗热汤

validation_note: 본문만으로 5문항+gist 재풀이해 정답 유일성 재확인. 한자수 160자(티어1 130~180 범위 내). scan answer '热汤/一碗热汤' 모두 본문 등장 확인. 초기 검증에서 발견한 결함: 보기 길이 편향(문항 4개+gist에서 정답이 유일 최장 → 최장 선택 편법 가능) → 오답 보기를 자연스럽게 늘려 재조정. 결과: 문항 중 유일-최장-정답 0개, gist 유일 최장 아님(정답 21자, 오답 22자 동률 이상). 오답 보기는 본문 근거 없음(눈·물건사기·수업 등). 기존 목록에 季节 주제 티어1 지문이 없어 신규 주제로 중복 없음.

title: 新老师的教学方法
text:
这个学期，班里来了一位新的数学老师，姓刘。刘老师上课的方法跟以前很不一样。以前的老师总是在黑板上写很多字，学生听着就行。刘老师却常常问问题，让学生自己想办法回答。

一开始，同学们不习惯，觉得比较累。可是过了几个星期，大家发现自己记住的东西多了，因为答案是自己想出来的，不容易忘。

上个星期考试，班里的成绩比上次好了很多。老师说，学习不是把书上的字背下来，而是要自己动脑筋。现在大家上数学课都更认真了。

questions.q: 这段话主要讲的是什么？
options: 刘老师的数学考试比以前难了很多
新老师用提问的方法帮助学生学习
同学们都不喜欢新来的刘老师
黑板上的字写得太多看不清
questions.explanation: 본문 전체가 류 선생님이 질문을 통해 학생 스스로 답을 찾게 했고, 그 결과 성적과 학습 태도가 좋아졌다는 내용이므로 2번이 정답입니다. 시험 난이도나 칠판 글씨는 주제가 아닙니다.

questions.q: 以前的老师上课时主要做什么？
options: 总是问学生很多问题让他们回答
在黑板上写很多字让学生听
让学生自己想办法找出答案
只让学生看书不讲课
questions.explanation: 본문에 '예전 선생님은 늘 칠판에 많은 글자를 썼고 학생은 듣기만 하면 됐다(以前的老师总是在黑板上写很多字，学生听着就行)'고 나오므로 2번이 정답입니다. 질문하기는 류 선생님의 방식입니다.

questions.q: 一开始，同学们为什么觉得上课比较累？

因为刘老师上课说话声音太小
因为要自己动脑筋想答案
因为每天的考试时间变长了

questions.q: 上个星期的考试结果怎么样？

title: 办公室里的新计划
text:
小林在一家小公司工作，主要负责回答客人的问题。以前每次有客人打电话来，他都要一个一个地找资料，常常要花很长时间。有些客人等得不高兴，就不想再买了。

经理知道以后，做了个新计划：把常见的问题和答案写成一张表，放在小林的电脑桌面上。这样小林一看表就能很快找到答案。

用了这个方法，小林回答问题快多了，客人也满意多了。上个月，公司还收到了几位客人写来的感谢信。经理很高兴，说以后想让别的同事也用这张表。

questions.q: 这段话主要讲的是什么？
options: 小林决定离开公司去换一份新工作
公司用一张问题表提高了效率
客人们都对这家公司很不满意
经理一直都不太喜欢小林
questions.explanation: 본문 전체가 문제-답변 표를 도입해 응대 속도와 고객 만족도가 좋아진 내용이므로 2번이 정답입니다. 이직·불만·호불호는 주제가 아닙니다.

questions.q: 以前小林回答客人问题时遇到了什么麻烦？
options: 公司的电话总是打不通接不上
要一个一个找资料花很长时间
客人们从来都不给公司打电话
公司里连一台电脑都没有
questions.explanation: 본문에 '매번 하나하나 자료를 찾아야 해서 오랜 시간이 걸렸다(一个一个地找资料，常常要花很长时间)'고 나오므로 2번이 정답입니다.

questions.q: 经理为什么决定做这个新计划？
options: 因为他想给公司省下更多的钱
因为客人等太久而不满意
因为小林向经理要求换工作
因为公司马上就要搬到别处
questions.explanation: 본문에서 고객이 오래 기다려 불만이 생기고 더는 사지 않으려 한 것을 경리가 알게 된 뒤 계획을 세웠으므로 2번이 정답입니다. 비용 절감·이직·이전은 본문에 없습니다.

questions.q: 经理让小林把常见问题和答案写成了什么？
options: 一本厚厚的说明书
一张表
一封长长的信
一个新的网站
questions.explanation: 본문에 '자주 나오는 질문과 답을 표 하나로 만들었다(把常见的问题和答案写成一张表)'고 명시되어 있으므로 2번이 정답입니다. 감사 편지가 나오지만 그것을 만든 것은 아닙니다.

questions.q: 从短文最后可以知道，经理接下来最可能做什么？
options: 把这个新计划取消掉不再用了
让别的同事也用这张表
把小林调到别的公司去
以后不再关心客人的感受
questions.explanation: 마지막 문장에서 경리가 '앞으로 다른 동료들도 이 표를 쓰게 하고 싶다(以后想让别的同事也用这张表)'고 말했으므로 2번이 정답입니다.

gist.q: 这段话主要讲了什么？
options: 公司用问题表提高了回答速度和满意度
小林一直都不太会使用电脑办公
经理最后决定把整个公司关掉
客人们都对公司感到很生气

scan.q: 公司上个月收到了什么？
scan.answer: 几位客人写来的感谢信

validation_note: 본문(177자, HSK3 티어1 범위)만으로 5문항+gist 정답이 유일합니다. 오답도 회사·고객·경리·전화·컴퓨터 등 본문 소재로 구체화하되 본문 근거가 없는 내용(전화 불통·이전·폐업 등)으로 만들었습니다. '정답=유일 최장'은 gist 1개뿐이고(글자수 재검), 나머지는 오답 길이를 정답과 맞춰 길이 단서를 없앴습니다. 원본 208자에서 트리밍. scan.answer '几位客人写来的感谢信'는 본문에 그대로 등장합니다.

title: 中秋节的晚上
text:
中秋节那天晚上，天气很好，月亮又大又圆。奶奶把桌子搬到院子里，放上月饼和水果，一家人坐在一起赏月。爷爷说，中秋节是家人团圆的日子，不管在哪里工作，这天都该回家。

哥哥今年在别的城市上班，因为工作忙，没能回来。他打来电话，说自己也买了月饼，一个人在宿舍里吃。他说，看着同一个月亮，就好像大家在一起。

奶奶笑着说，明年一定要让哥哥早点回来，一家人吃顿团圆饭。妹妹说，她已经想哥哥做的红烧肉了。院子里充满了欢声笑语。

questions.q: 这段话主要讲的是什么？
options: 一家人一起动手学着做月饼和水果
中秋夜一家人赏月并想念哥哥
哥哥今年换了一份新的工作岗位
爷爷和奶奶因为一点小事吵架了
questions.explanation: 본문 전체가 중추절 밤 가족이 함께 달을 감상하며 멀리 있는 형(오빠)을 그리워하는 내용이므로 2번이 정답입니다. 월병 만들기·이직·다툼은 본문에 없습니다.

questions.q: 奶奶把桌子搬到了哪里？
options: 家里的厨房
院子里
楼下的客厅
二楼的房间
questions.explanation: 본문에 '할머니가 탁자를 마당으로 옮겼다(奶奶把桌子搬到院子里)'고 나오므로 2번이 정답입니다.

questions.q: 爷爷说的「中秋节是家人团圆的日子」，最能说明什么？
options: 中秋节这天家里人都还要照常工作
爷爷希望家人这天能聚在一起
中秋节只有家里的老人才会过
爷爷其实一点也不喜欢过节日
questions.explanation: 할아버지가 중추절은 가족이 모이는 날이니 어디서 일하든 이날은 집에 와야 한다고 했으므로, 가족이 함께 모이길 바란다는 2번이 정답입니다.

questions.q: 哥哥今年为什么没能回家过中秋节？
options: 因为他不小心生病住院去了
因为工作太忙在别的城市上班
因为他一点也不喜欢吃月饼和水果
因为回家的路上一直都在堵车
questions.explanation: 본문에 '형(오빠)이 올해 다른 도시에서 일하는데 일이 바빠서 돌아오지 못했다(在别的城市上班，因为工作忙，没能回来)'고 명시되어 있으므로 2번이 정답입니다.

questions.q: 从短文可以看出，哥哥打电话时心里是什么感觉？
options: 虽然回不了家但心里还想着家人
他一点儿也不想念家里的任何人
他觉得自己一个人过节反而更好
他对家里的人感到非常生气
questions.explanation: 형(오빠)이 '같은 달을 보고 있으니 다 같이 있는 것 같다(看着同一个月亮，就好像大家在一起)'고 말한 데서 가족을 그리워하는 마음이 드러나므로 1번이 정답입니다.

gist.q: 这段话主要讲了什么？
options: 中秋夜一家人团聚赏月并想念哥哥
中秋节这天要吃很多很多的红烧肉
哥哥今年决定搬回老家来一起住
